Name: latency_aware_latent_inference
Title: Latency-Aware Inference in Latent Variable Models Using PAC-Bayes Principles
Short Hypothesis: Can PAC-Bayes frameworks enhance the efficiency and accuracy of latent variable models in low-latency inference settings, providing bounded overhead while minimizing training requirements?
Related Work: Existing literature, such as Ionides et al. (2015) and Kong et al. (2025), explores latent variable models and variational inference but lacks a focus on how PAC-Bayes principles can enforce latency constraints and enhance model performance in real-time applications. This proposal uniquely integrates PAC-Bayes theory with a latency-aware design, distinguishing it from previous works that focus primarily on either latent variable modeling or variational inference without explicit regard for real-time constraints.
Abstract: In this proposal, we explore the integration of PAC-Bayes principles into latent variable models to enable low-latency inference without compromising accuracy. Traditional approaches to latent variable modeling often overlook the necessity of computational efficiency in real-time applications. By employing PAC-Bayes frameworks, we can derive stability bounds that ensure strictly bounded inference overhead while minimizing training demands. This research aims to develop a Jacobian-based layer-skipping approach to facilitate this integration, allowing for adaptive inference strategies that can skip layers based on computational budgets. We will empirically validate our approach across various tasks, providing insights into the trade-offs between throughput and accuracy while ensuring compliance with latency constraints. This work not only contributes to the theoretical understanding of PAC-Bayes principles but also offers practical solutions for deploying latent variable models in latency-critical environments.
Experiments: Implement a baseline latent variable model using variational inference.
Develop a PAC-Bayes enhanced version of the model incorporating layer-skipping techniques.
Conduct experiments on standard datasets to evaluate the latency, accuracy, and throughput of both models.
Utilize metrics such as wall-clock time, accuracy (e.g., F1 score, AUC), and computational overhead to assess performance.
Analyze the sensitivity of the model's performance with different layer-skipping thresholds.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include the challenge of achieving desired accuracy levels while adhering to latency constraints. Additionally, the effectiveness of layer-skipping may vary across different types of latent variable models, and the empirical validation may require extensive tuning to ensure reproducibility across various datasets.
